data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery goes back to old name 'HBO Max' for streaming service
data.description:
Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) has adopted the old name "HBO Max" for its popular streaming service, the company announced at its Upfront event on Wednesday.

WBD started calling it "Max" in 2023, hoping that the shorter name would resonate more with families and children, but backpedaled on its decision after it realized the old name better represented the product. The decision was also influenced by changing consumer needs.

CEO David Zaslav said, "We are bringing back HBO, the brand that represents the highest quality in media, to further accelerate that growth in the years ahead."

"Returning the HBO brand into HBO Max will further drive the service forward and amplify the uniqueness that subscribers can expect from the offering," the company said in a statement.

The rebranding will be effective in summer 2025, the company said.

WBD launched HBO Max back in 2020. In its latest earnings report, the company said expanding the service's global footprint "remains a key priority" as it aims for a presence in over 85 markets by the end of 2025. It is also laying the groundwork for launches in the UK, Ireland, Italy, and Germany in early 2026.

The streaming business saw the addition of 5.3M subscribers, which brought WBD's total subscriber base to 122.3M (Bloomberg estimate 119.8M) at the end of Q1. Revenue from streaming was up 8% at $2.66B.

data.title: Sanara MedTech outlines 26% revenue growth and targets expansion to over 1,450 facilities by year-end 2025 while launching THP pilot
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sanara MedTech Inc. (SMTI) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Ron Nixon, CEO, highlighted that "our Sanara Surgical team delivered net revenue of $23.4 million, representing 26% growth year-over-year," attributing this to sales of soft tissue repair products, which increased to $20.5 million. Nixon also emphasized the 18% growth in bone fusion products, noting, "we saw strong growth in sales of both our CellerateRX Surgical and BIASURGE products, which continued to fuel our performance." He stated the company remains focused on commercial execution and is preparing to launch the Tissue Health Plus pilot program later in the second quarter.
  • Nixon reported advancements in the distributor network, expanded product pipeline through new technology distribution rights, and increased financial flexibility following amendments to the debt facility.
  • Seth Yon, President and Chief Commercial Officer, described the expansion to "over 400 distributor partners compared to over 250 of the first quarter of 2024 and over 350 at the end of 2024." He outlined plans to further onboard and activate distributor reps, increase surgeon penetration in existing facilities, and aim to sell into more than 1,450 facilities by the end of 2025.
  • Sam Muppalla, President and CEO of Tissue Health Plus, announced the launch of the first release of the THP technology platform, including the THP CoPilot app. Muppalla confirmed the platform "is being implemented with the wound care provider group in preparation for the launch of our first pilot program," with metrics focused on clinician protocol adherence, reduced administrative time, and operational improvements.
  • Elizabeth Taylor, CFO, stated, "First quarter gross profit increased $5 million, or 30%, to $21.6 million. Gross margin increased approximately 240 basis points to 92% of net revenue, driven primarily by lower manufacturing costs related to CellerateRX Surgical." She also noted the operating loss was $2.1 million and the net loss was $3.5 million, or $0.41 per diluted share.

Outlook

  • Taylor indicated, "Our team remains focused this year on delivering net revenue growth, driven by our Sanara Surgical segment. We continue to expect improvements in our Sanara Surgical segment profitability in 2025."
  • The company expects cash investment in Tissue Health Plus for H1 2025 to be approximately $7.5 million to $8.5 million, excluding the CarePICS acquisition.
  • Management reiterated no anticipated material tariff impact and continued pursuit of financial partners to support Tissue Health Plus execution.

Financial Results

  • Gross margin improved to 92% of net revenue, up 240 basis points year-over-year. Gross profit rose to $21.6 million.
  • Operating expenses climbed to $23.7 million, driven by a $5.2 million increase in SG&A, primarily from sales and marketing and infrastructure costs. Operating loss rose to $2.1 million.
  • Net loss was $3.5 million, or $0.41 per diluted share, with Sanara Surgical posting a $0.6 million net loss and Tissue Health Plus a $2.9 million net loss.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $0.7 million, with Sanara Surgical generating $2.7 million segment-adjusted EBITDA, up from $1.2 million last year.
  • Cash at quarter-end stood at $20.7 million, with $42.8 million in debt and $12.25 million available borrowing capacity.

Q&A

  • Unidentified Analyst (H.C. Wainwright) asked about penetration strategy and ROI from deeper engagement at existing facilities. Yon responded, "It's to get us access into new accounts, but also to penetrate existing accounts deeper and further. So it's really a coupled approach between both the RSM and our distributor partners."
  • Follow-up on reorder rates yielded the response from Yon: "We haven't provided that in the past... once surgeons gain comfort with those two products... they continue to use that and expand that across more procedures."
  • Matt Park (Cantor Fitzgerald) inquired about the sustainability of gross margin and future leverage. Nixon replied, "I can't see us achieving a lot more leverage than what we have today on the gross margin side."
  • Park also questioned THP pilot metrics. Muppalla specified, "We're really looking at the success of the pilot being measured in three sets of metrics. First is the clinician-facing metrics... second... increasing staff productivity... the third bucket of metrics we look at are adoption metrics."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone focused on penetration strategy, reorder rates, margin leverage, and THP pilot success, with a neutral to slightly positive sentiment and interest in operational details.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, highlighting growth and execution, and in Q&A, responded directly but did not disclose specific reorder data, showing a measured and steady sentiment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a consistent, constructive tone, with continued focus on commercial expansion and pilot metrics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, focused on revenue growth and segment profitability improvement.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward activating distributor partners and expanding facility penetration, with a new emphasis on onboarding and training in 2025.
  • Analysts’ focus stayed on penetration rates and pilot program metrics, similar to prior quarter interest in THP execution.
  • Gross margin improved quarter-over-quarter, and SG&A expenses increased, reflecting ongoing investments.
  • Management’s confidence in commercialization efforts and THP pilot remained steady, with no marked change in tone.

Risks and Concerns

  • Increased operating expenses, particularly in SG&A and infrastructure, were highlighted as a challenge by Taylor.
  • Tissue Health Plus segment continues to post significant losses and requires further investment; management is actively seeking financial partners to support this segment.
  • Management stated tariffs are not expected to have material impact, except for BioSurge products.
  • No specific new business risks or material operational headwinds were disclosed beyond continued investment needs.

Final Takeaway

Sanara MedTech delivered strong year-over-year revenue and gross margin growth, underpinned by expansion in distributor partnerships and new product launches. The company remains focused on deepening market penetration, onboarding and training distributor reps, and expanding into more than 1,450 facilities by year-end. With the launch of the THP technology platform and a pilot program set for Q2, management is confident in pursuing multiple growth opportunities and expects ongoing improvements in profitability for the Sanara Surgical segment in 2025, while continuing to seek financial partners to advance the Tissue Health Plus strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Retail watch: Dollar General is tipped by Evercore to see a near-term rally
data.description:
Evercore ISI added Dollar General Corporation (NYSE:DG) to its Outperform Tactical and Action Positioning Call List a few weeks ahead of the retailer's Q1 earnings report. 

The firm said Dollar General (NYSE:DG) appears to be driving turnaround traction based on Q1 comparable sales that it sees being +2% vs. a prior forecast for +1.2%. 

"Back to basics focus, improved processes, and potential for lower shrink are some key positives that, coupled with the sales momentum identified in our 2nd Measure data on Bloomberg, suggest modest upside to comp/EPS for Q1," highlighted analyst Michael Montani. 

Montani and his team are looking for a potential high single-digit to low double-digit pop in Dollar General (DG) shares over the next month on the view that the earnings release and guidance update will keep the stock grinding higher. 

With mid-single digit revenue growth seen as likely to return, Evercore ISI anticipates that Dollar General (DG) can win some multiple expansion from 14X toward a range of 15X to 17X, which implies a near-term trading range of approximately $95 to $100.

Shares of Dollar General (DG) were up 1.3% in premarket trading to $87.94 vs. the 52-week range of $66.43 to $147.87. The stock has been impacted over the last few weeks by investor concerns over tariffs and consumer discretionary spending.

data.title: Dynatrace targets 13–14% ARR growth to $1.99B in fiscal 2026 amid expanding AI and hyperscaler focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dynatrace (DT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Rick McConnell, CEO, highlighted a strong finish to fiscal 2025, noting "subscription revenue grew 20%. We surpassed $1.7 billion in ARR and $1 billion in DPS ARR. We expanded our non-GAAP operating margin by more than 100 basis points and our pre-tax free cash flow margin by roughly 250 basis points". He emphasized exceeding 4,000 customers, platform innovations including Grail for GCP, preventive operations, AI-powered log management, and being consistently named a leader in major analyst reports. McConnell stated, "we see observability spend continuing to be a priority," and expects "materially greater penetration in the coming year into hyperscaler workloads, where we expect the majority of observability market growth to occur".
  • McConnell outlined four growth drivers: ongoing go-to-market investments, DPS licensing broadening adoption, disruptive log management, and the agentic AI platform vision. He said, "the number of customers leveraging logs is up 18% compared to last quarter, plus nearly half of our new logos added in the fourth quarter are deploying logs in their initial implementation compared to roughly 20% in the same quarter last year." The addition of Steve McMahon as Chief Customer Officer was announced.
  • Jim Benson, CFO, stated, "Q4 was a strong finish to fiscal '25. Once again, we exceeded the high end of guidance across all top-line growth and profitability metrics." He highlighted that "annual recurring revenue, or ARR, ended the year at $1.73 billion, representing 17% growth, slightly above the high end of guidance." Benson added, "customer consumption growth rates are 2 times the rate of SKU-based customers and leading to much higher expansion rates."

Outlook

  • Dynatrace expects fiscal 2026 ARR to be between $1.975 billion and $1.99 billion, reflecting 13% to 14% growth. Total revenue is projected between $1.95 billion and $1.965 billion, up 14% to 15%. Subscription revenue guidance is $1.865 billion to $1.88 billion.
  • The company anticipates non-GAAP operating income of $560 million to $570 million and a non-GAAP operating margin of 29% for the year. Non-GAAP net income is projected at $481 million to $494 million, with non-GAAP EPS expected between $1.56 and $1.59 per diluted share.
  • Free cash flow is guided to $505 million to $515 million, or 26% of revenue. Management indicated, "we expect free cash flow to be significantly higher in the first and fourth quarters and significantly lower in the second and third quarters."
  • Q1 revenue is expected between $465 million and $470 million, with subscription revenue of $445 million to $450 million, both growing 16% to 17%. Q1 non-GAAP EPS is expected to be $0.37 to $0.38 per share.

Financial Results

  • ARR ended the year at $1.73 billion, up 17%. Total revenue for Q4 was $445 million, up 19%, with subscription revenue at $424 million, up 20%.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin for Q4 was 26%. Non-GAAP net income was $99 million or $0.33 per diluted share. Full year non-GAAP operating margin reached 29%, and free cash flow was $431 million or 25% of revenue.
  • On-demand consumption revenue in Q4 was $9 million, up from $7 million in Q3, contributing to subscription revenue growth. DPS customers now contribute over 60% of ARR, representing more than $1 billion.
  • The company repurchased 787,000 shares for $43 million in Q4.

Q&A

  • Patrick Colville, Scotiabank: Asked about quantitative context for logs and updates on the $100 million ARR target. Jim Benson responded, "we have high confidence we will exceed that in fiscal '26. And it is a business just to give you just some rough numbers, that business will grow well over 100% in fiscal '26." Rick McConnell added, "we like the metrics of more than $100 million in consumption this year... at that growth rate of north of 100%, we were quite optimistic about the business to come this year."
  • Matthew Hedberg, RBC: Inquired about sales productivity and go-to-market changes. Rick McConnell described partners as "a fundamental part of our strategy" and Jim Benson said, "we now have over 3/4 of our business that is leveraging a channel... we are adding what we're calling strike teams."
  • Brent Thill, Jefferies: Asked about 45% pipeline growth and close rates. Jim Benson noted, "the demand environment continues to be pretty resilient... there is a lag between consumption. And when you see it either show up in ARR or in subscription revenue through ODCs, but it is kind of a core underpinning of future growth for the company."
  • Rob Owens, Piper Sandler: Queried on unlocking the security opportunity. Rick McConnell indicated, "we see good traction with our RVA solution for vulnerability analytics... expanded product offerings... as well as expanded go-to-market."
  • Multiple analysts focused on the transition to DPS, on-demand consumption dynamics, and the impact on NRR and ARR, with management emphasizing growing consumption, the importance of DPS, and adoption-focused incentives.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions on the sustainability and predictability of on-demand revenue, DPS adoption, and growth levers. The tone was neutral to slightly positive, with a focus on clarifying metrics and seeking more quantitative guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we have high confidence" and "we feel very good about the traction." Some responses displayed caution regarding macro uncertainty and on-demand revenue predictability, but overall, management remained upbeat.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was increased emphasis on transition toward consumption models and AI-driven product differentiation, with both analysts and management showing higher confidence in long-term growth drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 ARR and revenue growth is lower than the previous quarter's outlook. Management is more explicit about the transition to DPS and on-demand models, highlighting their impact on reported ARR, NRR, and revenue.
  • The focus on AI, log management, and hyperscaler partnerships has intensified, with management outlining new platform releases and go-to-market initiatives.
  • Analysts continue to probe on the DPS model, on-demand consumption, and the effect on expansion rates and financial metrics, reflecting a sustained focus on understanding business model evolution.
  • Management's tone remains confident, with a stronger focus on operational execution and aligning incentives to consumption and adoption.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged an "uncertain economic environment" and "fluid nature of the geopolitical and macro landscape," stating, "we expect enterprises to remain careful in their spending, and our approach to guidance assumes an incremental level of caution."
  • The unpredictability of on-demand consumption revenue and the lag in translating consumption into committed ARR or NRR metrics were noted as challenges.
  • Management's mitigation efforts include conservative guidance assumptions for ODC, ongoing investments in customer success and strike teams, and a continued focus on partner engagement and platform innovation.

Final Takeaway

Dynatrace management underscored strong execution in fiscal 2025 and set a measured but optimistic outlook for fiscal 2026. The company is advancing its AI-powered platform and expanding into hyperscaler-driven growth, with a particular focus on consumption-based models and broadening customer adoption through DPS. While acknowledging macro uncertainties and the evolving nature of on-demand revenue, Dynatrace remains committed to driving innovation, operational efficiency, and long-term value through targeted investments and strategic partnerships.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hapag-Lloyd sees 'huge surge' in volume, as China-to-U.S. bookings bounce after tariff truce
data.description:
Shipper Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) (OTCPK:HLAGF) +11.6% in Frankfurt trading Wednesday after reporting higher Q1 earnings and reiterating full-year guidance, while cautioning that global trade conflicts and continued disruptions to the flow of commerce could affect supply and demand in the container shipping industry this year.

The world's fifth-largest container line by capacity reported Q1 EBITDA of €1.01B (~$1.13B), up from €835M in the year-earlier quarter and in line with company guidance of ~€1B, while revenues rose 19% to €5.05B, as volumes and average freight rates both rose by nearly 9% Y/Y due to strong demand.

Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) (OTCPK:HLAGF) said it still expects EBITDA of 2.4B-3.9B in 2025, but adding that volatile freight rates and major geopolitical challenges mean guidance is subject to considerable uncertainty.

The shipping industry was handed a reprieve earlier this week after the U.S. and China agreed to lower their tariffs for at least 90 days while the two sides continue talks, but the company said it remains unclear when passage through the Red Sea will be safe again while U.S. import tariffs have added considerable uncertainty among customers, initially weighing on demand.

Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) (OTCPK:HLAGF) is seeing a "huge surge in volume" this week after the U.S. and China lowered tariffs, CEO Rolf Habben Jansen told Bloomberg, as the pause in the trade war has unleashed a wave of pent-up demand for transpacific shipping.

Jansen estimated the boost at more than 50% compared with recent weeks, with bookings from China to the U.S., which had been down sharply during the past few weeks, rebounding strongly with other regions "all remaining pretty stable."

Major shipping lines including Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HPGLY) (OTCPK:HLAGF) have avoided sailing through the Red Sea since late 2023, and Jansen said companies "need visibility that it is safe and that it is going to remain safe for a longer period of time, because what we want to avoid at all costs is to go in and out."

data.title: B. Riley Financial unable to timely file quartery report on Form 10-Q
data.description:
B. Riley Financial (NASDAQ:RILY) said in a filing on Tuesday that it was unable to timely file its financial report on Form 10-Q for the quarter ended March 31.

The required filing date was May 12.

The company said it is working to complete the audit and finalize the annual report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2024.

B. Riley had said in November last year that it believes it can return to a normal process of filing financial reports starting in 2025.

data.title: Main Street Capital falls after short pitch at Sohn Investment Conf
data.description:
• Victor Bonilla, founder of Jehoshaphat Research, recommends shorting stock of Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) because a dividend cut is likely.
  • He presented his idea Wednesday at the Sohn Investment Conference in New York.
  • Main Street didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment. 
  • Shares of Main Street (NYSE:MAIN) fell 2.7%.

--reported by Rob Williams in New York.

data.title: Take-Two Q4 Earnings Preview: All Eyes on Grand Theft Auto VI
data.description:
Take-Two (NASDAQ:TTWO) is set to announce fourth quarter earnings on Thursday, and investors will watch out for the company’s net booking guidance for FY 2026 and updates on Grand Theft Auto VI.

Wall Street expects the video game maker to post EPS of $1.09, while revenue is expected to increase 10.7% Y/Y to $1.55 billion during the quarter.

The company forecasted net bookings for Q4 2025 to range from $1.48 billion to $1.58 billion, and said it expects record level of net bookings for fiscal 2026 and 2027 driven by Grand Theft Auto VI.

Rockstar Games, a Take-Two (NASDAQ:TTWO) game publishing unit, has uploaded a second trailer for Grand Theft Auto VI, days after announcing the delay of the release of the company's next blockbuster title.

Since its release in the nineties, Take-Two’s GTA has become one of the biggest franchises in the gaming world and has garnered millions of followers.

BofA reiterated its Buy rating on TTWO due to the reduced risk of another delay, and the greater likelihood that GTA 6 could launch with a price tag of over $70.

Jefferies also remained bullish about TTWO and increased its price target to $270 from $225.  The brokerage firm also updated its scenarios for GTA VI and moved base case FY27 GTA to 50 million console units, $4.2 billion total GTA bookings, and TTWO FY27 EPS of $10.58.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating System rated TTWO as a Strong Buy, with A+ rating in terms of growth and A rating in prospects of momentum.  Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street analysts are also bullish and rated the stock as Buy.

“Take-Two's robust release pipeline, including Civilization VII and Borderlands 4, supports management's forecast of record net bookings in FY 2026 and 2027,” argued Seeking Alpha analyst Simple Investment Ideas.

Over the last one year, TTWO has beaten EPS estimates 0% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 50% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have not seen any upward revisions, compared to two downward moves. Revenue estimates have seen two upward revisions versus no downward moves.

Shares of TTWO gained over 23% so far this year, outperforming the broader S&P 500 Index, which was flat during the same period.

data.title: Xenia announces additional $100M share repurchase authorization
data.description:
REIT Xenia Hotels & Resorts (NYSE:XHR) announced on Wednesday the authorization of up to an additional $100M share repurchase program.

The buybacks may be made in the open market and through privately negotiated transactions, or by other means.

As of May 13, the company had about $180M remaining under its total share repurchase authorization.

Additionally, Xenia Hotels declared $0.14/share quarterly dividend, in line with the previous.
